question_text: The pedigree method of plant breeding involves recording the parentage of each selected plant in the segregating generations following hybridisation, and is most commonly used in:
explanation: The pedigree method is widely used in self-pollinated crops (wheat, rice, soybean, chickpea) for handling segregating populations following hybridisation. The breeder selects superior individual plants from F₂ onwards and grows their progeny in plant-rows in F₃, recording detailed parentage of each selection. Selection continues until F₅-F₆ when the lines become essentially homozygous. Records of pedigree allow tracing of each line's ancestry. Other handling methods include bulk method (mass handling), SSD (single seed descent — one seed per plant), and modified bulk methods.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: Cross-pollinated crops use mass selection, recurrent selection or synthetic varieties — not pedigree method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing breeding methods for self vs cross-pollinated crops.

elimination_hints.hint: Vegetatively propagated crops use clonal selection; no segregating generations to handle by pedigree.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding the requirement for segregating generations in pedigree method.

elimination_hints.hint: Apomictic crops produce maternal clones without segregation; pedigree method is not applicable.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing apomixis with sexual reproduction requirements.

question_text: The Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee (GEAC), the apex regulatory body in India for approving the environmental release of genetically modified (GM) crops, functions under which Ministry?
explanation: The Genetic Engineering Appraisal Committee (GEAC) is the apex regulatory body in India for genetically modified organisms, functioning under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC). It was constituted under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 and Rules 1989. GEAC approves environmental release, large-scale field trials, and commercialisation of GM crops. It works alongside the Review Committee on Genetic Manipulation (RCGM) under DBT (which handles laboratory and contained research approvals). The Genetic Engineering Approval Committee was renamed in 2010 to current name — same acronym GEAC.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare
Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
Ministry of Health and Family Welfare

elimination_hints.hint: Ministry of Agriculture handles general crop policies, but GM regulation is under environmental jurisdiction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming agricultural regulation always falls under Ministry of Agriculture.

elimination_hints.hint: DBT regulates laboratory-stage genetic research through RCGM, but environmental release is GEAC under MoEFCC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing research-stage and environmental-release regulators.

elimination_hints.hint: Health Ministry regulates GM food safety through FSSAI, but environmental approval is GEAC under MoEFCC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing food safety regulation with environmental release approval.

question_text: The backcross method of plant breeding is used to transfer a single desirable gene (such as disease resistance) from a donor parent into an established adapted variety called the:
explanation: In the backcross method, the recurrent parent is the established, adapted, agronomically superior variety into which a desired trait (single gene like disease resistance) is to be transferred. The donor parent (or non-recurrent parent) is the source of the desired gene but is otherwise inferior. The F₁ hybrid is repeatedly crossed back to the recurrent parent for 5-7 generations (BC₁, BC₂... BC₆), each time selecting individuals carrying the target gene. After backcrossing, ~99% of the genome resembles the recurrent parent, with only the introgressed gene from the donor. This is widely used to add disease resistance to popular varieties.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: Donor parent provides the target gene but is the non-recurrent parent; it is not used repeatedly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing donor and recurrent parents in backcrossing.

elimination_hints.hint: Tester parent is used in test crosses to determine genotype, not in backcrossing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing test cross with backcross.

elimination_hints.hint: Maintainer parent is used in CMS-based hybrid seed production to multiply CMS lines, not in backcrossing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up hybrid seed production terminology with backcross terminology.

question_text: Marker-Assisted Selection (MAS) accelerates plant breeding by using molecular markers linked to genes of interest. Which of the following is the most widely used type of DNA marker in modern crop breeding?
explanation: Single Nucleotide Polymorphisms (SNPs) are now the most widely used DNA markers in modern plant breeding due to their high abundance throughout the genome (millions per crop), bi-allelic nature, suitability for high-throughput genotyping platforms (SNP arrays, KASP assays, sequencing), and amenability to automation. Other DNA markers include RFLP (older, labour-intensive), RAPD (random, less reproducible), AFLP (high multiplex but complex), and SSRs/microsatellites (highly polymorphic, still widely used). MAS uses these markers in marker-assisted backcrossing, marker-assisted recurrent selection, and genomic selection for traits like rust resistance and drought tolerance.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: RFLP is an older marker system, largely replaced by SNP and SSR markers in modern breeding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating older marker systems as currently dominant.

elimination_hints.hint: RAPD has low reproducibility and is rarely used in current breeding programmes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Choosing less reliable marker systems as widely used.

elimination_hints.hint: Allozymes are protein-based markers (not DNA) and are mostly historical, replaced by DNA markers.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing protein markers with DNA markers.

question_text: The somatic hybridisation technique fuses protoplasts (cells with cell wall removed) from two distantly related plant species. The resulting fusion product is called:
explanation: A cybrid (cytoplasmic hybrid) results when nuclei of two protoplasts fuse along with mixing of cytoplasms. However, when the term 'somatic hybrid' is used precisely, the product of complete protoplast fusion (containing nuclei and cytoplasm of both parents) is called a somatic hybrid. Both terms relate to protoplast fusion. The technique enables crossing of sexually incompatible species, e.g., creating Pomato (potato + tomato) and Brassicoraphanus (cabbage + radish). Steps include: enzymatic removal of cell wall (cellulase + pectinase), protoplast fusion using PEG or electrofusion, regeneration on selective medium, and confirmation of hybridity.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: Synthetic varieties are produced from random mating of selected parental lines, not protoplast fusion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing synthetic varieties with somatic hybrids.

elimination_hints.hint: Apomictic hybrids would arise without fertilisation; somatic hybrids result from physical cell fusion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up asexual reproduction with somatic hybridisation.

elimination_hints.hint: Reciprocal hybrids are sexual crosses with reversed parent roles, not cell fusion products.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing sexual reciprocal crosses with somatic fusion.

question_text: The Bulk method of plant breeding handles segregating populations after hybridisation by growing the entire F₂-F₅ populations together as bulks without individual selection until:
explanation: In the bulk method, segregating generations (F₂ to F₅ or F₆) are grown together as a population without individual plant selection. Natural selection eliminates inferior types over generations. Around F₆-F₇ when populations become essentially homozygous, individual plant selection begins to identify superior lines. Advantages include: simplicity (less labour and record-keeping), allows natural selection, and conserves genetic variability for later use. Disadvantages include: superior types may be lost to natural selection (e.g., shorter plants outcompeted by taller ones in early generations) and longer time to identify desired genotypes.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: F₃ generation onwards with detailed records
F₆-F₇ when populations are essentially homozygous
Throughout all generations without any selection

elimination_hints.hint: Selection at F₂ alone is the pedigree method's starting point, not the bulk method.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pedigree and bulk methods.

elimination_hints.hint: Detailed records from F₃ describe the pedigree method, which is the opposite of the bulk method's approach.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing pedigree and bulk method characteristics.

elimination_hints.hint: Selection must eventually occur to identify and release the superior line as a variety.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating bulk method as having no selection ever.

question_text: The Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights Act (PPV&FR Act) was enacted in India in which year to grant intellectual property rights for new plant varieties while protecting farmers' rights?
explanation: The Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers' Rights (PPV&FR) Act was enacted by the Indian Parliament in 2001 to comply with India's TRIPS obligations under WTO while uniquely protecting farmers' rights. It establishes a sui generis system different from the international UPOV convention. The Act is administered by the PPV&FR Authority (set up in 2005). Key features include: registration of new varieties, extant varieties, farmers' varieties, and essentially derived varieties; farmers can save, use, sow, exchange and even sell seeds of protected varieties; provides disclosure of geographical origin and traditional knowledge; offers a 'Farmer Award' for conservation contributions.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: 1991 was the year of UPOV revision; India enacted its own Act later (2001).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing international and Indian PVP timelines.

elimination_hints.hint: 2005 is the year the PPV&FR Authority was established to implement the 2001 Act.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the Act's enactment year with the Authority's establishment year.

elimination_hints.hint: By 2010, the Act was already in force; this is too late for the enactment year.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the Act's enactment date.

question_text: Distant or wide hybridisation involves crossing of plants belonging to different species or genera. The major problem in successful production of distant hybrids is:
explanation: Hybrid sterility is the major bottleneck in distant hybridisation. F₁ hybrids between distantly related species often have unbalanced chromosome sets (different chromosome numbers/structures) preventing normal pairing and segregation during meiosis. This produces non-viable gametes leading to sterility. Sterility can be partial (low seed set) or complete. Sterility is overcome by chromosome doubling using colchicine (creating fertile amphidiploids like Triticale), embryo rescue, ovule culture, or backcrossing. Other distant hybridisation problems include cross-incompatibility (failure of fertilisation) and seed inviability requiring embryo rescue techniques.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Excessive vigour of F₁ hybrids leading to lodging
Hybrid sterility due to unbalanced chromosome sets
Higher disease resistance making selection difficult
Identical phenotypes of both parents preventing identification

elimination_hints.hint: Hybrid vigour is generally desired in breeding; lodging is a separate trait, not the main distant hybridisation problem.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating beneficial hybrid vigour as a problem.

elimination_hints.hint: Disease resistance is a goal of distant hybridisation, not a problem.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing breeding objectives with breeding problems.

elimination_hints.hint: Distant hybridisation involves clearly distinct parents; their phenotypes are usually quite different.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing close and distant hybridisation parent characteristics.

question_text: Single Seed Descent (SSD) method differs from the bulk method in handling segregating populations because in SSD:
explanation: In Single Seed Descent (SSD), one randomly chosen seed is taken from each F₂ plant and bulked to produce F₃. The same procedure (one seed per plant) continues through F₃, F₄, F₅ until populations approach homozygosity (F₅-F₆). At that stage, individual plant selection begins. Key advantages: (1) maximum genetic diversity is maintained as each F₂ plant contributes equally; (2) no selection bias from the breeder; (3) generations can be advanced rapidly in greenhouse without spacing requirements; (4) suits crops where field testing is expensive. SSD differs from bulk method (where natural selection acts on whole populations) and pedigree method (where individual selection starts at F₂).
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Multiple seeds from each plant are bulked together
Detailed pedigree records are maintained in every generation
One single seed from each plant is advanced to the next generation
Selection of superior plants begins from F₂ onwards

elimination_hints.hint: Multiple seeds per plant is the bulk method approach, not SSD.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing SSD with bulk method.

elimination_hints.hint: Detailed pedigree records describe the pedigree method, not SSD.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing SSD with pedigree method.

elimination_hints.hint: Early F₂ selection describes the pedigree method; SSD postpones selection until homozygosity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating SSD as similar to pedigree in selection timing.

question_text: The first transgenic crop officially approved for commercial cultivation in India was Bt cotton in the year 2002. Bt brinjal (eggplant), although developed and tested, has not been commercialised due to concerns from:
explanation: Bt brinjal received GEAC approval for environmental release in October 2009 but was placed under indefinite moratorium by the then Environment Minister Jairam Ramesh in February 2010 due to extensive public consultations revealing concerns about: long-term safety on human health and biodiversity (India is the centre of brinjal diversity with hundreds of landraces); inadequate independent testing; opposition from farmer groups, scientists and civil society; and the precautionary principle. Bt cotton remains the only commercial GM crop in India. Bangladesh approved Bt brinjal in 2013 and continues cultivation. India's GM food crop debate continues with HT mustard also under similar moratorium since 2017.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Insufficient yield improvement in field trials
High cost of seed making it commercially unviable
Public concerns over biosafety and biodiversity
Lack of approval from the international community

elimination_hints.hint: Bt brinjal field trials showed effective pest resistance; the moratorium was based on safety, not yield.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing efficacy issues with biosafety issues.

elimination_hints.hint: Cost was not the moratorium reason; the issue was public consultation and safety concerns.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing economic with safety regulatory concerns.

elimination_hints.hint: International approval is not a regulatory requirement; India makes sovereign decisions through GEAC.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misunderstanding India's independent regulatory framework.

question_text: Match the polyploid crops in Column A with their type/origin in Column B and select the correct option from the choices given below.
explanation: Each crop has a specific polyploid origin: Bread wheat (Triticum aestivum) is an allohexaploid (2n=6x=42, AABBDD genomes from three diploid wild ancestors); Cotton (Gossypium hirsutum) is an allotetraploid (2n=4x=52); Banana (Musa) cultivated varieties are mostly autotriploid (2n=3x=33) producing parthenocarpic seedless fruits; Potato (Solanum tuberosum) is an autotetraploid (2n=4x=48). Polyploidy is more common in plants than animals and contributes to crop productivity through gene dosage and heterozygosity effects.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: Bread wheat is hexaploid (6x), not autotetraploid (4x).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing hexaploid wheat with tetraploid potato.

elimination_hints.hint: Bread wheat is allohexaploid (3 different genomes), not allotetraploid like cotton.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up wheat and cotton polyploid types.

elimination_hints.hint: Bread wheat is hexaploid, not triploid like banana.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing different polyploid levels in crops.

question_text: Match the biotechnology terms/tools in Column A with their function or application in Column B and select the correct option.
explanation: Each biotechnology tool has a specific function: Vector (Ti plasmid, viral vectors) carries the gene of interest into the host cell; Restriction enzymes (EcoRI, HindIII) cut DNA at specific recognition sequences acting as molecular scissors; DNA ligase joins/seals DNA fragments together with phosphodiester bonds, acting as molecular glue; PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) amplifies specific DNA sequences exponentially using primers, Taq polymerase and thermal cycling. Together these tools enable cloning, gene transfer and DNA analysis in modern plant biotechnology.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

column_b.text: Cuts DNA at specific recognition sequences
Carries the gene of interest into host cell

elimination_hints.hint: Vectors carry genes; cutting DNA is the function of restriction enzymes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing vector function with restriction enzyme function.

elimination_hints.hint: Vectors do not join DNA fragments; that is DNA ligase's role.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing vectors with ligases.

elimination_hints.hint: Vectors do not amplify DNA; that is PCR's function.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up cloning and amplification tools.

question_text: Match the Indian agricultural revolutions in Column A with the crop/sector they transformed in Column B and select the correct option.
explanation: India's agricultural revolutions transformed specific sectors: Green Revolution (1960s-70s) — wheat and rice productivity through HYV varieties, irrigation and fertilisers; White Revolution (Operation Flood, 1970s-90s) — milk production through dairy cooperatives; Yellow Revolution (1980s-90s) — oilseed production (mustard, sunflower, soybean); Blue Revolution (1985 onwards) — fish and aquaculture production. Other revolutions include Pink (prawns/onions), Brown (leather/cocoa), Round (potato), Silver (eggs), and the ongoing Evergreen Revolution focusing on sustainable productivity.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

column_b.text: Wheat and rice productivity through HYVs

elimination_hints.hint: Green Revolution focused on wheat and rice (cereals), not milk production.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Green Revolution with White Revolution.

elimination_hints.hint: Green Revolution did not focus on oilseeds; that is Yellow Revolution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Green and Yellow Revolutions.

elimination_hints.hint: Green Revolution focused on cereals, not on fish/aquaculture (Blue Revolution).
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up different colour-coded revolutions.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: Marker-Assisted Selection (MAS) is more efficient than conventional phenotypic selection for transferring disease resistance genes.
reason: Molecular markers linked to resistance genes can be detected at the seedling stage in laboratory without exposing plants to actual disease pressure.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. MAS allows breeders to identify gene-carrying plants at seedling stage by genotyping for linked DNA markers, eliminating the need for disease screening in field nurseries (which requires presence of pathogen, suitable environment, expert disease scoring, and is destructive). MAS is especially advantageous for: recessive genes, traits expressed late in life cycle, traits with low heritability or expensive phenotyping, and when pyramiding multiple resistance genes. Examples include MAS for bacterial blight resistance (Xa21) in rice and rust resistance (Lr34) in wheat.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: Early seedling-stage detection without disease pressure is precisely the source of MAS efficiency.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link the marker advantage with selection efficiency.

elimination_hints.hint: Marker-based detection at seedling stage is a well-documented advantage of MAS.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established methodology of MAS.

elimination_hints.hint: MAS efficiency for disease resistance breeding is well established and widely practised globally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the established efficiency advantage of MAS.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: Synthetic varieties are commonly used in the improvement of cross-pollinated crops like maize, sunflower and pearl millet.
reason: Synthetic varieties are produced by random mating of several genetically diverse but compatible parental lines or clones, maintaining heterozygosity and high yield potential over generations.
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Synthetic varieties (Syn-1, Syn-2, etc.) are developed by inter-mating selected superior parents (typically 4-10 inbred lines or clones) chosen for their general combining ability (GCA). The seeds are bulked and farmers can save and reuse seed for several generations without significant yield decline (unlike F₁ hybrids where yield drops sharply in F₂). This is particularly valuable for cross-pollinated crops in marginal regions where farmers cannot afford fresh hybrid seed annually. Synthetic varieties serve as a middle ground between open-pollinated varieties and F₁ hybrids.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: Random mating maintaining heterozygosity is the precise mechanism that makes synthetics work for cross-pollinated crops.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link random mating with sustained heterozygosity.

elimination_hints.hint: Random mating from selected diverse parents is the well-established procedure for synthetic varieties.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established procedure for synthetic varieties.

elimination_hints.hint: Synthetic varieties of maize, sunflower and pearl millet have been successfully developed and released globally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the established commercial use of synthetic varieties.

question_text: Read the following Assertion (A) and Reason (R) carefully and select the most appropriate option from the choices given below.
assertion: F₁ hybrids show maximum heterosis but farmers cannot save and reuse the seed in subsequent seasons.
reason: In the F₂ generation derived from selfing of F₁ hybrid plants, segregation of genes leads to genetic variability and substantial reduction in yield (~30-50%).
explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. F₁ plants are uniformly heterozygous (high heterosis = high yield). When farmers save and replant F₂ seeds, segregation breaks up the heterozygous combinations — giving a mix of homozygous and heterozygous plants with much lower mean yield than the uniform F₁. This is called inbreeding depression in F₂. As a result, farmers must purchase fresh F₁ hybrid seed each season from seed companies, ensuring a continuous market for hybrid seed industry. This biology drives the economics of hybrid seed sector in maize, vegetables, sunflower and rice.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
A is true, but R is false.
A is false, but R is true.

elimination_hints.hint: F₂ genetic segregation and yield reduction is precisely the reason farmers cannot reuse hybrid seed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to link F₂ segregation with the inability to save hybrid seeds.

elimination_hints.hint: F₂ segregation and yield decline (~30-50%) is well documented in hybrid crops.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the established F₂ yield reduction phenomenon.

elimination_hints.hint: The maximum heterosis of F₁ hybrids is one of the most established facts in plant breeding.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the well-known F₁ heterosis.

question_text: Arrange the following steps of the pedigree method of plant breeding in their correct chronological sequence following hybridisation of two parents.
explanation: The pedigree method follows a structured sequence: (1) F₁ generation — uniform heterozygous plants, all bulked together (no selection); (2) F₂ generation — maximum segregation, individual superior plants selected based on phenotype; (3) F₃ to F₅ — progeny of each F₂ selection grown in plant-rows, recording detailed pedigree, with continued individual selection within superior families; (4) F₆-F₇ — selected lines reach near-homozygosity, evaluated in replicated yield trials at multiple locations, and best lines are nominated for variety release. This method is ideal for self-pollinated crops following hybridisation.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

items.text: F₆-F₇: Replicated yield trials at multiple locations
F₁: Bulking of all uniform hybrid plants without selection
F₃-F₅: Plant-row progeny grown with detailed pedigree records
F₂: Individual superior plant selection from segregating population

elimination_hints.hint: Yield trials (F₆-F₇) are the final stage, not the first; F₁ is the starting point.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the entire breeding sequence.

elimination_hints.hint: F₂ selection cannot precede F₁ generation; F₁ must come first as the hybrid generation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping F₁ as the starting generation.

elimination_hints.hint: F₃-F₅ plant-rows cannot precede F₁ hybrid generation in the breeding sequence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Misordering the breeding generations.

question_text: Arrange the following steps in marker-assisted backcrossing (MABC) for transferring a disease resistance gene from a donor parent to an elite recurrent parent in their correct sequence.
explanation: Marker-assisted backcrossing follows a precise sequence: (1) Identification of the donor parent carrying the target resistance gene and the elite recurrent parent (RP); (2) Crossing the donor with RP to produce the F₁ hybrid carrying both genomes; (3) Backcrossing the F₁ to the RP and using foreground markers (linked to resistance gene) to select BC₁F₁ plants carrying the target gene; (4) Continuing backcrossing for 2-3 more generations using both foreground markers (for target gene) and background markers (to recover maximum RP genome) until the gene-carrying line has ~99% RP genome with the introgressed resistance. Final selfing fixes the gene in homozygous condition.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

items.text: Continue 2-3 backcrosses with foreground and background markers
Identify donor parent (with gene) and elite recurrent parent
Cross donor with recurrent parent to produce F₁ hybrid
First backcross F₁ × RP; select BC₁F₁ with foreground markers

elimination_hints.hint: Backcrossing cannot occur before parents are identified and crossed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the entire breeding sequence.

elimination_hints.hint: F₁ production cannot precede parent identification; parents must be selected first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Skipping the parent identification step.

elimination_hints.hint: BC₁F₁ generation cannot precede F₁ hybrid; hybridisation must come before backcrossing.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing F₁ and BC₁F₁ in the sequence.

question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: India was the first country in the world to commercially cultivate genetically modified Bt cotton, having approved it in the year 2002.
explanation: The statement is false. India was NOT the first country to commercialise Bt cotton. The United States approved and commercialised Bt cotton in 1996 — six years before India's 2002 approval. China followed in 1997, and Australia, South Africa, Argentina also approved Bt cotton before India. India was however the largest cotton-producing country and Bt cotton transformed Indian cotton cultivation, making India the largest producer with over 90% area now under Bt cotton hybrids. The first GM crop globally was the Flavr Savr tomato (USA, 1994).
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: USA commercialised Bt cotton in 1996, six years before India's 2002 approval.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming India was the first country to commercialise Bt cotton.

elimination_hints.hint: The chronology of Bt cotton commercialisation is well-documented historical fact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating well-documented chronology as uncertain.

elimination_hints.hint: The 2002 India approval date is correct, but the 'first country' claim is wrong, making the statement false overall.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a partially correct factual statement as fully correct.

question_text:
Study the following table comparing different methods of handling segregating populations in self-pollinated crops, and answer the question that follows.

| Method | Selection Starts | Genetic Diversity | Records Maintained | Time to Variety |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pedigree | F₂ onwards | Reduced early | Detailed pedigree | 10-12 years |
| Bulk | F₆-F₇ | Maintained | Minimal | 12-15 years |
| Single Seed Descent | F₅-F₆ | Maximum | Minimal | 8-10 years (greenhouse) |
| Backcross | After backcrossing | Restricted to target gene | Generation records | 6-8 years |

Based on the table, which method maintains the maximum genetic diversity in segregating generations and can be most rapidly advanced through greenhouse/off-season nursery cultivation?
explanation: Single Seed Descent (SSD) maintains maximum genetic diversity because every F₂ plant contributes equally one seed to the next generation, with no breeder selection bias and minimal natural selection. SSD can be most rapidly advanced through greenhouses and off-season nurseries because plants need only produce a single seed (no need for full agronomic spacing, replicated trials or yield assessment in early generations). This allows multiple generations per year, often shortening variety development to 8-10 years versus 12-15 years for bulk method. SSD is particularly valuable for handling many crosses simultaneously and for crops where conventional generation advance is slow.
subject: agriculture
chapter: Agrometeorology, Genetics and Plant Breeding, Biochemistry and Microbiology

elimination_hints.hint: Pedigree method reduces diversity through early selection from F₂ onwards.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing pedigree's early selection with diversity maintenance.

elimination_hints.hint: Bulk method maintains diversity but takes longer (12-15 years), not rapid; SSD is faster.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing diversity maintenance with rapid advancement.

elimination_hints.hint: Backcross method restricts diversity to the target gene region only, opposite to maximum diversity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing focused gene transfer with broad diversity maintenance.
